From a single-chart swing strategy to a portfolio-level trading engine that reports its own P&L, symbol by symbol and timeframe by timeframe
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Page 1 — Why Most ZigZag Strategies Never Leave the Chart They Were Built On
Swing-based breakout strategies are one of the oldest ideas in technical trading. Price forms a high, price forms a low, and when a new close pushes beyond the last confirmed swing, momentum is assumed to be continuing in that direction. The ZigZag indicator is the classic tool for finding those swing points automatically, filtering out the minor wiggles that would otherwise trigger false signals.
The problem isn’t the idea. The problem is that almost every retail implementation of this strategy — including most of the ones sold as “backtest case studies” — stops at a single instrument on a single timeframe. You get an equity curve for EUR/USD on H1, or ETH-USD on daily candles, and it looks great in isolation. What you don’t see is:

• How the same logic behaves on ten different symbols at once

• Whether the edge survives when you’re not hand-picking the one timeframe that…
